title: EXPOSURE XIV THEOREM OF FINITUDE FOR AN OWN MORPHISM; COHOMOLOGICAL DIMENSION OF AFFINE ALGEBRIC SCHEMES

pages.title: EXPOSURE XIV THEOREM OF FINITUDE FOR AN OWN MORPHISM; COHOMOLOGICAL DIMENSION OF AFFINE ALGEBRIC SCHEMES

blocks.html: <h1 id="XIV">PRESENTATION
Summary

1. Finiteness theorem for a proper morphism
2. A variation of the dimension
3. Cohomological dimension of affine algebraic schemes
4. Proof of Theorem 3.1
This talk contains two important theorems which are proved using the change of base theorem for a proper morphism.

pages.title: 1. Finiteness theorem for a proper morphism.

blocks.html: 1. Finiteness theorem for a proper morphism.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.1

Let \(f : X \to S\) be a clean morphism of finite presentation. Let \(A\) be a left Noetherian ring which is a suitable \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-algebra for \(n \in \mathbb{N}\). Let \(F\) be a bundle of sets (resp. groups, resp. \(A\)-modules) constructible on \(X\). Then the beam \(f_* F\) (resp. \(R^1 f_* F\), resp. \(R^q f_* F\) for each \(q \geqslant 0\)) is also constructible.
In particular, we have:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.2

Let \(X\) be a proper diagram on the spectrum of a separably closed body \(k\), and let \(F\) be an abelian torsion beam constructible on \(X\). Then the groups \(H^q(X,F)\) are finite for each \(q \geqslant 0\).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 1.3

The theorem is false for abelian sheaves if we omit the condition that \(f\) is clean, as we see by taking \(F = \mathbb{Z}/p\), \(p = \operatorname{car} k\), \(X = \mathbb{E}^1_k\), the affine space, and \(f\) the inclusion of \(X\) in \(\mathbb{P}^1_k\), or the structural morphism \(X \to \operatorname{Spec} k\). However, we conjecture that it is true if \(F\) is prime to the residual characteristics of \(S\) and \(S\) is "excellent" (EGA IV 7.8). We can demonstrate it, when we have the theorem for resolving singularities (for example in zero characteristics) for an excellent scheme \(S\) of equal characteristics (XIX 5.1).

Demonstration of 1.1. The assertion is local to \(S\) and we can therefore take \(S\) affine. Then \(S\) is the limit of ring spectra of finite type on \(\mathbb{Z}\), and the data of the theorem are such that we can obtain them, by change of base \(S \to S_o\), of a proper morphism \(f_o : X_o \to S_o\) with \(S_o\) of finite type on \(\mathbb{Z}\) and a constructible beam \(F_o\) on \(X_o\), cf. EGA IV 8 and IX 2.7.4. Now, since the inverse image of a constructible sheaf is still constructible (IX 2.4 (iii)), we are brought back, by applying XII 5.1 to the base change morphism \(S_o \leftarrow S\), to demonstrate the constructibility of \(f_{o*} F_o\) (resp. of \(R^q f_{o*} F_o\)), hence the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.4

Just check 1.1 with \(S\) of finite type on \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{Z}\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.5

The set assertion of 1.1 is true, i.e. \(f_* F\) is a constructible sheaf of sets if \(F\) is.

Demonstration. We can assume \(S\) Noetherian (1.4). Let \(F \hookrightarrow G = \prod_i \pi_{i*}(C_i)\) be an injection with \(\pi_i : X_i \to X\) finite and \(C_i\) constant and constructible on \(X_i\) (IX 2.14). We have \(f_* F \subset f_* G\) and consequently (IX 2.9 (iii)) it suffices to treat the case \(F = G\). Now \(f_* G = \prod_i (f \pi_i)_* C_i\), and a finished product of beams constructible on \(S\) is constructible (IX 2.14). By replacing \(X\) by \(X_i\), we are brought back to the case where \(F = D_X\) is a constructible constant beam, with value \(D\). Now by virtue of XII 5.1 (i), the fiber \(f_*(F)_{\bar{s}}\) is isomorphic to \(D^{C(\bar{s})}\), where \(C(\bar{s}) = \pi_o(X_{\bar{s}})\), so it is finite. According to IX 2.13 (iii) it is enough to demonstrate that the function "number of elements of the fiber of \(f_* F\)" is constructible on \(S\). Now this function is \(\operatorname{card}(D^C)\), where \(C\) is the function "number of connected components in the geometric fiber", and \(C\) is constructible (EGA IV 9.7.9), hence the result.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.6

Let \(S\) be a Noetherian schema, \(f : X \to S\) proper, \(F\) a bundle of groups (resp. of \(A\)-modules) constructible on \(X\), \(q\) integer equal to \(0\) or \(1\) (resp. an integer \(\geqslant 0\)). Let \(S_i\) \((1 \leqslant i \leqslant n)\) be subschemas of \(S\) such that \(S = \bigcup_i S_i\) and let \(F_i\), \(f_i : X_i \to S_i\) be the "restrictions" of the data to \(S_i\). So if for all \(i\), \(R^q f_{i*} F_i\) is constructible, it is the same for \(R^q f_* F\).
Demonstration. The restriction of \(R^q f_* F\) to \(S_i\) is isomorphic to \(R^q f_{i*} F_i\) by XII 5.1, and the lemma follows from IX 2.8.
We now proceed with reasoning similar to that of XII 8:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.7

Let \(S\) be Noetherian,

\[\xymatrix{ X \ar[dr]_{f} & & \bar{X} \ar[ll]_{\pi} \ar[dl]^{\bar{f}} \\ & S & }\]

a commutative diagram of proper morphisms, and \(j : U \to X\) an open such that the open \(\bar{U} = U \times_X \bar{X}\) is sent isomorphically to \(U\). Let \(Y = X - U\) be the closed reduced scheme and let \(f_o : Y \to S\) and \(i : Y \to X\) be the canonical morphisms. Let Theorem 1.1 hold for \(\bar{f}\), \(f_o\) and \(\pi\). Then it is also true for \(f\).
Demonstration. Let us first deal with the case of a bundle of \(A\)-modules \(F\) on \(X\). We have the exact sequence

and taking into account the exact corresponding cohomology sequence for the \(R^q f_*\), and IX 2.6, we see that it is enough to check the constructibility of the \(R^q f_*\) for the extreme members (note that \(i_* i^* F\) is constructible according to IX 2.4 (ii) and IX 2.15 (i), therefore \(j_! j^* F\) is also (IX 2.6)). Since \(i\) is a closed immersion,

(VIII 5.6), hence the constructibility in this case according to the hypothesis on \(f_o\). Moreover, denoting by \(\bar{j} : \bar{U} \to \bar{X}\) the inclusion morphism and \(\bar{F} = \pi^* F\), we have

as we check by first defining an arrow \(\to\), and proving that it is invertible fiber by fiber using XII 5.1 (i). At each geometric point \(\bar{y}\) of \(Y\), the restriction of \(\bar{j}_! \bar{j}^* \bar{F}\) to the fiber \(\bar{X}_{\bar{y}}\) of \(\bar{X}\) on \(X\) is zero, and consequently XII 5.1 (iii) the fiber of \((R^q \pi_*)(\bar{j}_! \bar{j}^* \bar{F})\) in \(\bar{y}\) is zero for each \(q \geqslant 0\). Since on the other hand \(\pi\) induces an isomorphism above \(X - Y\), we have \((R^q \pi_*)(\bar{j}_! \bar{j}^* \bar{F}) = 0\) if \(q > 0\). From Leray's spectral suite
\[E_2^{p,q} = (R^p f_*)(R^q \pi_*)(\bar{j}_! \bar{j}^* \bar{F}) \Longrightarrow R^n \bar{f}_*(\bar{j}_! \bar{j}^* \bar{F})\]
and \((*)\) we deduce isomorphisms
\[(R^q \bar{f}_*)(\bar{j}_! \bar{j}^* \bar{F}) \simeq (R^q f_*)(j_! j^* F)\]

for \(q \geqslant 0\). Since \(\bar{j}_! \bar{j}^* \bar{F}\) is constructible (same reasoning as for \(j_! j^* F\)) the right-hand side is also constructible, hence the constructibility of \((R^q f_*)(j_! j^* F)\) and therefore of \(R^q f_* F\).

Now suppose that \(F\) is a bundle of groups constructible on \(X\). Let's replace \(\pi : \bar{X} \to X\) with \(\pi \amalg i : (\bar{X} \amalg Y) \to X\). It is thus sufficient to demonstrate that if \(\pi : \bar{X} \to X\) is surjective and if 1.1 is true for \(\pi\) and for \(\bar{f}\), it is also true for \(f\). Let first \(\bar{F}\) be a bundle of groups constructible on \(\bar{X}\). Then \(\pi_* \bar{F}\) is constructible on \(X\) (1.5) and we have an injection \((R^1 f_*) \pi_* \bar{F} \hookrightarrow (R^1 \bar{f}_*)\bar{F}\). The right-hand side is constructible by hypothesis, and consequently the left-hand side is also (IX 2.9 (ii)). Now since \(\pi\) is surjective, we have an injection \(F \to \pi_* \pi^* F = G\). The beam \(\bar{F} = \pi^* F\) is constructible, therefore \(G\) and \(R^1 f_* G\) are also constructible, as we saw above. We are thus brought back to the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.8

Let \(S\) be Noetherian, \(f : X \to S\) be a proper morphism, and \(F \overset{u}{\hookrightarrow} G\) be an injection of sheaves of constructible groups on \(X\). If \(R^1 f_* G\) is constructible, \(R^1 f_* F\) is too.
Demonstration. Let \(C = G/F\) which is a constructible bundle of homogeneous spaces under \(G\) (apply IX 2.6). Consider the exact sequence
\[f_* C \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } R^1 f_* F \xrightarrow{\ u^1\ } R^1 f_* G \tag{\(*\)}\]

To demonstrate that \(R^1 f_* F\) is constructible, it suffices by Noetherian induction and 1.6 to demonstrate that if \(S \neq \emptyset\), there exists a non-empty open of \(S\) on which \(R^1 f_*(F)\) is constructible. We can therefore assume \(R^1 f_* G\) locally constant, so since constructibility is a local notion for the sparse topology, we can assume that \(S\) is irreducible and \(R^1 f_* G\) is constant (and constructible), at value \(E = \{e_1,\ldots,e_n\}\). Let \(D_i\) \((i = 1,\ldots,n)\) be the sub-bundle of \(R^1 f_* F\) inverse image of the section \(e_i\) by \(u^1\). We have, since in a topos "the sums are universal", \(R^1 f_* F = \amalg_i D_i\), and it is therefore sufficient to demonstrate that each \(D_i\) is constructible on a suitable \(S'\) spread over \(S\) and non-empty.

Recall that \(R^1 f_* F\) is the beam associated with the pre-beam \(\hat{R}^1 F\), where \(\hat{R}^1 F(S') = H^1(X',F)\). Consequently we can assume (by replacing \(S\) by a non-empty \(S'\) spread over \(S\)) that \(D_i\) is the "empty" beam (therefore constructible), or that there exists an element \(\alpha \in H^1(X,F)\) which induces a section \(\alpha\) of \(D_i\). In the latter case, either
\[f_* C^\alpha \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } R^1 f_* F^\alpha \longrightarrow R^1 f_* G^\alpha\]
the exact sequence deduced from \((*)\) "by twisting using \(\alpha\)". We have
\[R^1 f_* F^\alpha \simeq R^1 f_* F \;, \qquad R^1 f_* G^\alpha \simeq R^1 f_* G \;,\]

and the section of \(R^1 f_* F^\alpha\) corresponding to \(\bar{\alpha}\) is the unit section. Therefore the sub-beam \(D_i^\alpha\) of \(R^1 f_* F^\alpha\) corresponding to \(D_i\) is the inverse image of the unit section of \(R^1 f_* G^\alpha\), i.e., \(D_i^\alpha\) is the image of \(f_* C^\alpha\) by \(\delta\). Since \(f_* C\) is constructible (1.5) it is the same for \(D_i^\alpha\), therefore for \(D_i\) (apply IX 2.6), hence the lemma.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.9

Let \(S\) be Noetherian and

\[\xymatrix{ X \ar[dr]_{f} & & \bar{X} \ar[ll]_{\pi} \ar[dl]^{\bar{f}} \\ & S & }\]

a commutative diagram of proper morphisms. If Theorem 1.1 is true for \(f\) and \(\pi\), it is also true for \(\bar{f}\).
Demonstration. In the case of a constructible beam \(\bar{F}\) of \(A\)-modules, we use the Leray spectral sequence
\[E_2^{p,q} = (R^p f_*)(R^q \pi_*)\bar{F} \Longrightarrow (R^n \bar{f}_*)\bar{F} \;.\]
By hypothesis, we find that \(E_2^{p,q}\) is constructible for each \(p,q\), and consequently the outcome is also, as then follows from IX 2.6.
Let \(\bar{F}\) be a constructible bundle of groups. We have the exact sequence (XII 3.2)

where the extreme members are constructible according to the hypothesis and 1.5. We proceed as in the demonstration of 1.8: It suffices by Noetherian recurrence and 1.6 to assume \(S \neq \emptyset\), and to demonstrate constructability on a suitable non-empty open space. We can therefore assume \(f_*(R^1 \pi_*)\bar{F}\) locally constant, and (by equal localization) even constant and constructible (IX 2.8) at value \(E = \{e_1,\ldots,e_n\}\). Let \(D_i\) be the sub-beam of \((R^1 \bar{f}_*)\bar{F}\) inverse image of \(e_i\), so that \((R^1 \bar{f}_*)\bar{F} = \amalg_i D_i\). It suffices to demonstrate that each of the beams \(D_i\) induces a constructible beam on a suitable \(S'/S\) connected and non-vacuum plane. If \(D_i\) is not the "empty beam", we come back to the case where there exists an element \(\alpha \in H^1(\bar{X},\bar{F})\) which induces a section \(\bar{\alpha}\) of \(D_i\). Either
\[0 \to (R^1 f_*) \pi_* \bar{F}^\alpha \to (R^1 \bar{f}_*)\bar{F}^\alpha \to f_*(R^1 \pi_*)\bar{F}^\alpha\]

the exact sequence deduced from \((*)\) by twisting \(\bar{F}\) using \(\alpha\). The section of \((R^1 \bar{f}_*)\bar{F}^\alpha\) corresponding to \(\bar{\alpha}\) is the unit section, and consequently the sub-beam \(D_i^\alpha\) of \((R^1 \bar{f}_*)\bar{F}^\alpha\) corresponding to \(D_i\) is the inverse image of the unit section of \(f_*(R^1 \pi_*)\bar{F}^\alpha\), hence \(D_i^\alpha \simeq (R^1 f_*) \pi_* \bar{F}^\alpha\), which is indeed a constructible beam, cqfd.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.10

It is enough to check 1.1 in the case where \(S\) is of finite type on \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{Z}\), and \(f\) is projective and of relative dimension \(\leqslant 1\).

Demonstration. Let us first assume that \(f\) is projective and let us proceed by induction on the relative dimension of \(f\), which we can assume to be finite, even if it means replacing \(S\) by an open affine. If \(f\) is of relative dimension \(\leqslant n\), with \(n \geqslant 2\), we can find, locally on \(S\), a finite morphism \(\pi : X \to \mathbb{P}^n_S\), and we return by 1.9 and IX 2.15 (i) in the case where \(f\) is the morphism structural of \(\mathbb{P}^n_S\). Consider the commutative diagram of morphisms
\[\xymatrix{ & \bar{X} \ar[dl] \ar[dr]^{a} \ar[dd]|(0.35){\bar{f}} & \\ \mathbb{P}^n_S \ar[dr]_{f} & & \mathbb{P}^1_S \ar[dl]^{b} \\ & S & }\]

already considered in the proof of XII 8.3, where \(\bar{X}\) is deduced from \(\mathbb{P}^n_S\) by exploding the closed subschema \(Y \simeq \mathbb{P}^{n-2}_S\). The relative dimension of \(b\) is \(\leqslant 1\), that of \(a\) is \(\leqslant n-1\), and consequently the theorem is true for \(a\) and \(b\) by the induction hypothesis, therefore for \(\bar{f}\) by (1.9). Additionally, the dimension of \(\bar{\pi}\) is \(\leqslant 1\), that of \(f_o : Y \to S\) is \(\leqslant n-1\). It therefore follows from 1.7 that 1.1 is true for \(f\), therefore for any projective morphism. Now let \(f\) be arbitrary, and let us proceed by Noetherian induction on \(X\): we can assume the theorem is true for each closed subschema distinct from \(X\) and that \(X \neq \emptyset\). Either
\[\xymatrix{ X \ar[dr]_{f} & & \bar{X} \ar[ll]_{\pi} \ar[dl]^{\bar{f}} \\ & S & }\]
a diagram of the type given by Chow's lemma XII 7.1. By applying 1.7 to this diagram, we deduce 1.1 for \(f\), hence the lemma.

1.11. We can now complete the demonstration of 1.1. If \(F\) is a bundle of constructible groups, we can find an injection \(F \hookrightarrow G = \prod_i \pi_{i*} C_i\) with \(\pi_i\) finite and \(C_i\) constant and constructible (IX 2.15), and it follows from 1.8 that we can assume \(F\) constant. Likewise, if \(F\) is a constructible bundle of \(A\)-modules, we can, by applying IX 2.15, find a resolution \(F \to G^\bullet\), \(G^\bullet = \{0 \to G^0 \to G^1 \to G^2 \to \cdots\}\), of \(F\), such that each \(G^\nu\) is of the form \(G^\nu = \prod_i \pi_{i*} C_i\), and we subsequently reduce the spectral resolution of \(G^\bullet\),
\[E_2^{p,q} = H^p\big((R^q f_*)G^\bullet\big) \Longrightarrow (R^n f_*)F \;,\]
in case \(F\) is constant and constructible, with value \(M\).
Recall that \(A\) is a \(\mathbb{Z}/n\)-algebra. Let \(\ell\) be a prime number that divides \(n\). We have the exact sequence
\[0 \to \ell M \to M \to M/\ell M \to 0 \;,\]
and it is still enough to prove 1.1 for \(\ell M\) and \(M/\ell M\).

We then reduce, by an easy recurrence, to the case \(A\) is a \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell\)-algebra, \(\ell \in \mathbb{P}\), and where \(M\) is a \(A\)-module of finite type. Then \(M\) is a vector space over \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell\), and hence the canonical morphism
\[H^q(Y,\mathbb{Z}/\ell) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}/\ell} M \to H^q(Y,M)\]
is bijective, whatever \(Y\) quasi-compact and quasi-separate (indeed, it is trivial if \(M\) is of finite rank, and the two members commute at inductive limits (VI 1.6)), therefore
\[\big[R^q f_*(\mathbb{Z}/\ell)\big] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}/\ell} M \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } R^q f_*(M) \;.\]

It is therefore obviously sufficient to demonstrate that \(R^q f_*(\mathbb{Z}/\ell)\) is constructible as a \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell\)-Module, that is to say, we are reduced to the case \(A = \mathbb{Z}/\ell\), which we assume from now on.
Note that the case where \(f\) is of relative dimension \(\leqslant 0\) (i.e. where \(f\) is finite) and \(F\) is arbitrary now results from VIII 5.6 and 1.5.

Let's proceed by Noetherian recurrence on \(S\). According to 1.6, we can assume \(S \neq \emptyset\), and allow ourselves to replace \(S\) by any non-empty open. Furthermore, we can assume \(S\) is integrated, with generic point \(s\). The fiber \(X_s\) is an algebraic scheme of dimension \(\leqslant 1\), and consequently there exists a root extension \(K'\) of \(k(s) = K\), such that the normalized \(Y\) of \((X_s \otimes_K K')_{\text{réd}}\) is smooth above \(\operatorname{Spec} K'\)(∗). By replacing \(S\) by a non-empty open, one can suppose that there exists a surjective finite root morphism \(S' \to S\) such that \(S'_s\) is \(K\)-isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Spec} K'\), and a surjective finite morphism, a smooth and projective morphism \(\bar{X}' \to S'\) and a \(S'\)-surjective finite morphism \(\pi : \bar{X}' \to X' = X \times_S S'\) which induces \(Y \to X_s \otimes_K K'\) on the fibers at the generic point \(s\) of \(S\) (EGA IV 9.6.1). But according to VIII 1.1 it is harmless to make a root extension. We can therefore replace \(S\) by \(S'\) and \(X\) by \(X'\), that is to say, we can assume that \(f\) fits into a diagram
\[\xymatrix{ X \ar[dr]_{f} & & \bar{X} \ar[ll]_{\pi} \ar[dl]^{\bar{f}} \\ & S & }\]

where \(\pi\) is surjective and finite, and \(\bar{f}\) is smooth. Furthermore, from the construction, one can assume that there exists a closed subscheme \(Y\) of \(X\) such that the generic fiber of \(Y\) is finite, and such that \(\pi\) induces an isomorphism above \(X - Y\). By again replacing \(S\) by a non-empty open, we return to the case where furthermore \(f_o : Y \to S\) is a finite morphism.

As \(F\) is constant, \(\pi^* F\) is also constant, and \(F \to \pi_* \pi^* F\) is injective because \(\pi\) is surjective. If \(F\) is a bundle of groups, it suffices to demonstrate that \((R^1 f_*)(\pi_* \pi^* F) \simeq (R^1 \bar{f}_*)(\pi^* F)\) is constructible (1.8). If \(F\) is a bundle of \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell\)-modules, we have an exact sequence
\[0 \to F \to \pi_* \pi^* F \to C \to 0 \;,\]

where \(C\) is focused on \(Y\), because \(\pi\) induces an isomorphism above \(X - Y\). It follows that \(R^q f_* C = R^q f_{o*} C\) is constructible (null if \(q > 0\)), and so it is still sufficient to demonstrate that \((R^q f_*)(\pi_* \pi^* F) = (R^q \bar{f}_*)(\pi^* F)\) is constructible. By replacing \(f\) by \(\bar{f}\) and \(F\) by \(\pi^* F\), we are reduced to the case \(F\) constant and constructible and \(f\) is smooth.

Let us treat the case of a constant sheaf of groups, \(F = G_X\). All fibers in \((R^1 f_*)G_X\) are finished; in fact, by applying XII 5.2 (ii) we are reduced, to prove it, to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of a separably closed body and where \(X\) is smooth and of dimension \(\leqslant 1\) above \(S\). The result is known in this case (SGA 1 X 2.6). It is therefore sufficient to demonstrate that the specialization morphisms are injective (IX 2.13 (ii)). For this, we are reduced to the strictly local case \(S\), and by the base change theorem XII 5.1 (ii) we can even replace \(S\) by the spectrum of a discrete and strictly local valuation ring. Furthermore, we can assume \(X\) related.

Since \(H^1(X,G)\) classifies the main Galois coverings of \(X\) of group \(G\), it suffices to verify that if \(X' \to X\) is a connected flat covering then the geometric fiber is also connected. This easily results from the fact that \(f\) is smooth and clean, with connected geometric fibers.

Now let's deal with the case \(F = (\mathbb{Z}/\ell)_X\). For values \(0\) And \(1\) of \(q\) the result is already known ((1.5) and above). According to X 4.3 and XII 5.3 bis, only the case remains \(q = 2\), and moreover, if \(S\) is characteristic \(\ell\), we have \(R^2 f_*(\mathbb{Z}/\ell) = 0\). By replacing \(S\) by a suitable non-empty open, we are therefore reduced to the case where \(\ell\) is invertible on \(S\). Then, according to XII 5.2 (ii) and X 4.7, the fiber of \(R^2 f_*(\mathbb{Z}/\ell)\) at a geometric point \(\bar{s}\) above \(s\) is isomorphic to \((\mathbb{Z}/\ell)^{c(s)}\), Or \(c(s)\) is the number of irreducible components of dimension \(1\) geometric fiber \(X_{\bar{s}}\). Gold \(c\) is a function constructible on \(S\) (EGA IV 9.7.9), hence the result (IX 2.13 (iii)).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 1.11

The proof of 1.1 is greatly simplified by using the formalism of cohomology with proper supports, which will be developed in XVII as a direct consequence of the base change theorem for a proper morphism XII 5.1. On the other hand, statement 1.1 generalizes to the case where we take on \(Y\) any bundle of torsion rings \(\underline{A}\), and we take on \(X\) a complex \(K^\bullet\) of \(f^*(\underline{A})\)-Modules satisfying a condition of "constructibility" which, in the case particular 1.1, would be expressed simply by saying that cohomology sheaves \(\mathscr{H}^i(K^\bullet)\) of \(K^\bullet\) are constructible \(A\)-modules, zero for \(i\) large enough.
2. A variation of the dimension.
2.1. Let \(X\) be a schema. We will note
\[d(x) = \dim \overline{\{x\}} \tag{2.1.1}\]
for \(x \in X\), where \(\overline{\{x\}}\) is the stickiness of \(\{x\}\). If \(F\) is an abelian sheaf on \(X\), we set
\[d(F) = \sup \{d(x) \mid x \in X \text{ et } F_{\bar{x}} \neq 0\} \;. \tag{2.1.2}\]
We will need a corrected notion in the local case:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.2

Let \(Y\) be the spectrum of a universally catenary Noetherian local ring (EGA IV 5.6.2) and \(f : X \to Y\) be a morphism of finite type. Let \(x \in X\) and \(y = f(x)\). We pose

\[\delta(x) = \dim \overline{\{y\}} + \operatorname{deg.\,tr.} k(x)/k(y) \;.\]

If \(F\) is an abelian sheaf on \(X\), we set

\[\delta(F) = \sup \{\delta(x) \mid x \in X \text{ et } F_{\bar{x}} \neq 0\} \;.\]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.3
  1. (i) Let \(i : X' \to X\) be an immersion and \(x' \in X'\). So \(\delta(i(x')) = \delta(x')\).
  2. (ii) Let \(X_o\) be the closed fiber of \(X/Y\). We have \[\delta(x) = \begin{cases} d(x) & \text{si } \overline{\{x\}} \cap X_o \neq \emptyset \\ d(x)+1 & \text{si } \overline{\{x\}} \cap X_o = \emptyset \;; \end{cases}\]
  3. (iii) \(\delta(x) = d(x)\) if \(f\) is clean.

Demonstration. Assertion (i) is trivial from the definition. Since \(\overline{\{x\}} \cap X_o\) is not empty in the proper case \(f\), (iii) is a consequence of (ii). For (ii), we can replace \(X\) by \(\overline{\{x\}}\) and \(Y\) by \(\overline{\{y\}}\) (with the induced structure reduced), which is allowed according to (i). Let \(x' \in X\) and \(y' = f(x')\). We have the formula (EGA IV 5.6.1.1)
\[\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,x'} = \dim \mathcal{O}_{Y,y'} + \operatorname{deg.\,tr.} k(x)/k(y) - \operatorname{deg.\,tr.} k(x')/k(y') \;.\]
Assume \(X_o \neq \emptyset\). Then it is clear that \(\sup_{x'} \{\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,x'}\} = \dim X\) is obtained by taking for \(x'\) a closed point of \(X_o\), hence
\[\dim X = \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,x'} = \dim Y + \operatorname{deg.\,tr.} k(x)/k(y) = \delta(x) \;.\]

If \(X_o = \emptyset\), the supremum is obviously obtained by taking for \(x'\) a closed point in its fiber \(f^{-1}(y')\), such that \(\dim \overline{\{y'\}} = 1\). There are such points, because the \(y' \in Y\) with \(\dim \overline{\{y'\}} = 1\) form a very dense set (EGA IV 10.1.3, 10.5.9, 1.8.4). We therefore have:
\[\dim X = \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,x'} = \dim Y - 1 + \operatorname{deg.\,tr.} k(x)/k(y) = \delta(x) - 1 \;,\]
hence the result.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.4

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a morphism of finite type schemas on the spectrum of a body \(k\). Let \(x \in X\), \(y = f(x)\), and \(y_o\) be a specialization of \(y\). Let \(f' : X' \to Y'\) be the strict localization of \(f\) at a geometric point \(\bar{y}_o\) above \(y_o\), and \(x' \in X'\) a point above \(x\). So we have

\[\delta(x') = d(x) - d(y_o) \;.\]
Demonstration. We have
\[d(x) = d(y) + \operatorname{deg.\,tr.} k(x)/k(y)\] \[\delta(x') = d(y') + \operatorname{deg.\,tr.} k(x')/k(y') \;,\]
where \(y' = f'(x')\). In addition
\[\operatorname{deg.\,tr.} k(x)/k(y) = \operatorname{deg.\,tr.} k(x')/k(y') \;,\]
because the extensions \(k(x')/k(x)\) and \(k(y')/k(y)\) are algebraic. It therefore remains to demonstrate that
We can assume \(y\) maximum in \(Y\), therefore \(y'\) maximum in \(Y'\), and then

\[\begin{aligned} d(y') &= \dim \mathcal{O}_{Y',\bar{y}_o} \\ &= \dim \mathcal{O}_{Y,y_o} && \text{(EGA IV 6.1.3)} \\ &= \dim Y - d(y_o) && \text{(EGA IV 5.2.3.1)} \\ &= d(y) - d(y_o) \;, \end{aligned}\]
hence the result.

pages.title: 3. Cohomological dimension of affine algebraic schemes.

blocks.html: 3. Cohomological dimension of affine algebraic schemes.

blocks.label: 3.1 (d)
blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.1 (d)

Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an affine morphism of finite type schemas on a body \(k\). Let \(F\) be a torsion beam on \(X\) such that \(d(F) \leqslant d\) (cf. 2.1). So \(d(R^q f_* F) \leqslant d-q\).
In particular, taking \(Y = \operatorname{Spec}(k)\):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.2

Let \(X\) be an affine scheme of finite type on a separably closed body \(k\). So

\[\operatorname{cd} X \leqslant \dim X \;.\]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.3

Let \(X \subset \mathbb{P}^N_k\) be a projective schema of dimension \(n\) on a separably closed body \(k\), and let \(Y = H \cap X\) be a "hyperplane section" of \(X\). Then for any torsion beam \(F\) on \(X\), the canonical homomorphism

\[H^q_Y(X,F) \longrightarrow H^q(X,F)\]

is bijective for \(q > n+1\), and surjective for \(q = n+1\).

The corollary is trivial from 2.2 and from the exact sequence for a closed subset (IV 3.7.1), given that \(X - Y = \mathcal{U}\) is affine of dimension \(\leqslant n\). Note that 3.3 is a generalization of one of Lefschetz's theorems on hyperplane sections: in fact, if \(X\) is smooth on \(k\) and \(F\) is locally constant and prime to the characteristic, we can calculate the cohomology \(H^q_Y(X,F)\) explicitly using the purity theorem relative cohomology (XVI 3) and the spectral sequence ( ), and we find that
\[H^q_Y(X, \mathbb{Z}/n) \simeq H^{q-2}(Y, \mu_n^{-1}) \quad \text{pour tout } q \;;\]
the homomorphism \(H^{q-2}(Y, \mu_n^{-1}) \to H^q(X, \mathbb{Z}/n)\) deduced from 3.3 being none other than the "Gysin homomorphism", which will be studied later(∗).
Theorem 3.1 (d) is equivalent to the following:

blocks.label: 3.4 (d)
blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4 (d)

Let \(Y'\) be a strictly local scheme which is a strict localization of an algebraic scheme on a body. Let \(f' : X' \to Y'\) be an affine morphism of finite type, and \(F'\) be a torsion beam on \(X'\) such that \(\delta(F') \leqslant d\) (cf. (2.2)). Then \(H^q(X',F') = 0\) for \(q > d\).

Indeed, suppose that 3.4 (d) is true. Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an affine morphism of algebraic schemes on \(k\) and let \(F\) be a torsion beam on \(X\) such that \(d(F) \leqslant d\). Let \(y\) be a point in \(Y\) and \(\xi\) be a geometric point above \(y\). To prove 3.1 (d), it is necessary to show that the fiber \((R^q f_* F)_\xi\) is zero if \(d(y) > d-q\).
Let \(f' : X' \to Y'\) be the strict localization of \(f\) at the point \(\xi\), that is to say, let \(Y'\) be the strict localization of \(Y\) at \(\xi\) and let \(X' = X \times_Y Y'\).
Let \(F'\) be the induced beam of \(F\) on \(X'\), so that \((R^q f_* F)_\xi = H^q(X',F')\) (VIII 5.2). We have
\[\delta(F') \leqslant d(F) - d(y) \leqslant d - d(y) \tag{2.4}\]
and applying 3.4 (d), we find \(H^q(X',F') = 0\) if \(q > d - d(y)\), hence the result.

Conversely, suppose that 3.1 (d) is true, and let \(F'\), \(f' : X' \to Y'\) be as in the statement of 3.4 (d). Since the cohomology commutes at inductive limits (VII 3.3) and \(F'\) is the inductive limit of its constructible sub-sheaves (IX 2.9), we can assume \(F'\) constructible. Now \(Y'\) is strictly localized of an algebraic scheme \(Y\) at a geometric point \(\xi\) above a closed point \(y_o\) (X 3.3). Let us write \(Y' = \varprojlim_\alpha Y_\alpha\), where \((Y_\alpha)\) is a projective filtering system of affine schemes spread over \(Y\) (VIII 4.5). According to IX 2.7.4, \(F'\) is induced from a constructible torsion beam on \(X_\alpha = X \times_Y Y_\alpha\) for \(\alpha\) sufficiently large, and we immediately see, using 2.4 and EGA IV 9.5.5, that we can further assume that \(d(F_\alpha) = \delta(F') \leqslant d\). By replacing \(Y\) by \(Y_\alpha\), we are reduced to the case where \(F'\) is induced by a beam \(F\) on \(X\), with \(d(F) \leqslant d\). We then have \(R^q f_* F = 0\) if \(q > d\), according to 3.1 (d), therefore in particular
\[(R^q f_* F)_\xi = H^q(X',F') = 0 \quad \text{si } q > d,\]
hence 3.4 (d).
As a special case of 3.4 (d), we have

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.5

Let \(Y'\) be a strictly local scheme of dimension \(\leqslant d\), strictly localized of an algebraic scheme on a body \(k\). Let \(U \subset Y'\) be an open affine. So \(\operatorname{cd} U \leqslant d\).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 3.6

It seems very plausible that 3.4, therefore 3.5, remains valid for any local Noetherian scheme \(Y'\) (not necessarily a strict localization of an algebraic scheme), at least if \(Y'\) is excellent (EGA IV 7.8). This is what will be proven in (XX 6) when we further assume \(Y\) of zero characteristic.

footnotes.html: (∗) Cf. SGA 2 XIV for a systematic study of theorems of the Lefschetz type.

blocks.html: 4. Proof of Theorem 3.1.
The demonstration is done by induction on \(d\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.1

3.1 (0) and 3.1 (1) are true.

Demonstration. Trivial for \(d = 0\), in which case \(\operatorname{supp} F\) is finite on \(k\), and we apply (VIII 5.5). Let \(F\), \(f : X \to Y\) as in the statement of 3.1 and suppose that \(d(F) \leqslant 1\). By replacing \(F\) by a constructible sub-beam (IX 2.9 and VII 3.3), we reduce ourselves to the case where \(F\) is constructible. Then the support of \(F\) is contained in a closed subschema of \(X\) of dimension \(\leqslant 1\) (IX 2.3 (ii)), and we can replace \(X\) by this subschema, that is to say, we can assume that we have \(\dim X \leqslant 1\). According to IX 5.7, the cohomological dimension of an affine algebraic scheme of dimension \(1\) over a separably closed field is \(\leqslant 1\). It is also sufficient to treat the case where \(k\) is separably closed: in fact, let \(\bar{f} : \bar{X} \to \bar{Y}\) be the morphism deduced from \(f\) by the change of base \(\operatorname{Spec} \bar{k} \to \operatorname{Spec} k\) (\(\bar{k}\) the separable closure of \(k\)). For a geometric point \(\xi\) of \(Y\), the strict localization \(f' : X' \to Y'\) of \(f\) in \(\xi\) is identified with a strict localization of \(\bar{f}\), therefore the fiber \((R^q f_* F)_\xi\) with a fiber of \(R^q \bar{f}_* \bar{F}\). Since \(f\) is affine, we therefore find that \(R^q f_* F = 0\) if \(q > 1\). Furthermore, it is obvious that \(d(f_* F) \leqslant 1\). For the case \(q = 1\), note that the hypothesis that \(X\) is of dimension \(\leqslant 1\) implies that there exists a finite closed part \(Z\) of \(X\) such that \(f\) is finite above \(Y - Z\). It follows that \(R^1 f_* F\) is zero in \(Y - Z\) (VIII 5.6), hence \(d(R^1 f_* F) \leqslant 1\), which completes the proof of the lemma.
Let \(d \geqslant 2\) and now suppose that 3.1 (d') (or, which amounts to the same thing, 3.4 (d')) is true for \(d' < d\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.2

To prove 3.4 (d) in all cases, it suffices to treat the case where \(X' = \mathbb{E}^1_{Y'}\) is the affine space of dimension \(1\) over \(Y'\), and where \(f'\) is the structural morphism.

Demonstration. Obviously, we can assume \(X' = \mathbb{E}^N_{Y'}\), for \(N\) suitable, because we can dive \(X'\) into a \(\mathbb{E}^N_{Y'}\). Suppose \(N > 1\) and the result known for all \(Y'\) and for \(\mathbb{E}^r_{Y'}\), with \(r < N\). Let \(F'\) be a torsion beam on \(\mathbb{E}^N_{Y'}\), with \(d(F') \leqslant d\). Consider the diagram
\[\xymatrix{ \mathbb{E}^N_{Y'} \ar[rr]^{g'} \ar[dr]_{f'} & & \mathbb{E}^{N-1}_{Y'} \ar[dl]^{h'} \\ & Y' & }\]

\(g'\) and \(h'\) the canonical projections, so that \(\mathbb{E}^N_{Y'}\) is isomorphic to the affine space of dimension \(1\) above \(\mathbb{E}^{N-1}_{Y'}\). Since obviously each strict localized ring of \(\mathbb{E}^{N-1}_{Y'}\) is also the strict localized of an algebraic scheme, we can apply the recurrence hypothesis to the fibers of \(R^q g'_* F'\), and we find that
\[\delta(R^q g'_* F') \leqslant d-q \;. \tag{\(*\)}\]
Moreover, 3.4 (d') is true for \(h'\), \(d' \leqslant d\), by the induction hypothesis on \(N\).
Consider the Leray spectral sequence
\[E_2^{p,q} = H^p\big(\mathbb{E}^{N-1}_{Y'}, R^q g'_*(F')\big) \Longrightarrow H^n\big(\mathbb{E}^N_{Y'}, F'\big) \;.\]
We have \(E_2^{p,q} = 0\) if \(p > d-q\), as we find by applying \((*)\) and 3.4 (d') to the morphism \(h'\). It follows that the outcome is zero if \(n > d\), hence the lemma.
Consider the following additional statement, which is a special case of 3.5:

blocks.label: 4.3 (d)
blocks.title: Statement
blocks.html:
Statement 4.3 (d)

Let \(X'\) be strictly local of dimension \(\leqslant d\), strict localized of an algebraic scheme on a body. Let \(f \in \Gamma(X', \mathcal{O}_{X'})\), and let \(U \subset X'\) be the open \(U = X' - V(f)\). So \(\operatorname{cd} U \leqslant d\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.4

4.3 (d) implies 3.4 (d).
Demonstration. Consider the diagram
\[\xymatrix{ \mathbb{E}^1_{Y'} \ar[rr]^{i} \ar[dr]_{f'} & & \mathbb{P}^1_{Y'} \ar[dl]^{g'} \\ & Y' & }\]

where \(Y'\) is as in the statement of 2.6 and where \(\mathbb{P}^1_{Y'}\) is the projective space, deduced from \(\mathbb{E}^1\) "by adding the section \(Y'_\infty\) to infinity". Let \(F'\) be a torsion beam on \(\mathbb{E}^1\) with \(\delta(\operatorname{supp} F') \leqslant d\). We have the spectral sequence
\[H^p(\mathbb{P}^1, R^q i_* F') \Longrightarrow H^n(\mathbb{E}^1, F') \;.\]

But the \(R^q i_* F'\), for \(q > 0\), are concentrated on \(Y'_\infty\), which is \(Y'\)-isomorphic to \(Y'\). Since \(Y'\) is strictly local, it follows that \(H^p(Y'_\infty, R^q i_* F') = 0\) if \(p,q > 0\). Moreover, according to the base change theorem for a proper morphism (XII 5.1), the cohomology of a torsion beam on \(\mathbb{P}^1\) can be calculated on the closed fiber, which is a cohomological dimension scheme \(\leqslant 2\) (X 4.3). We therefore have \(H^p(\mathbb{P}^1, i_* F') = 0\), if \(p > 2\). As we want to demonstrate \(H^n(\mathbb{E}^1, F') = 0\) for \(n > d\), and as \(d \geqslant 2\), we are reduced to demonstrating that \(H^0(\mathbb{P}^1, R^q i_* F') = 0\) for \(q > d\). This group, also isomorphic to \(H^0(Y'_\infty, R^q i_*(F'))\), is by virtue of (VIII 4.6) isomorphic to the fiber of \(R^q i_* F'\) at the point \(Q\), \(Q\) being the point at infinity of \(\mathbb{P}^1\) in the closed fiber.

It is enough (VII 3.3 and IX 2.9) to treat the case where \(F'\) is constructible. Then, since \(\delta(\operatorname{supp} F') \leqslant d\), there exists a closed subschema \(X'\) of \(\mathbb{P}^1\) with \(\dim X' \leqslant d\), such that \(X'\) contains support for \(F'\). Let \(\widetilde{X}'\) be the localized of \(X'\) at the geometric point \(Q\), or \(\widetilde{U} = \widetilde{X}' - \widetilde{X}' \times_{\mathbb{P}^1} Y'_\infty\), and let \(F\) be the beam induced by \(F'\) on \(\widetilde{U}\) by the morphism \(\widetilde{U} \to \mathbb{P}^1\). Then the fiber from \(R^q i_* F\) to the point \(Q\) is none other than \(H^q(\widetilde{U},F)\) (VIII 5.2), and we apply 4.3 (d) to the strictly local scheme \(X'\) and to the open \(\widetilde{U}\).
The lemma below, attached to 4.4, will complete the proof.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.5

3.4 (d') for \(d' < d\) implies 4.3 (d') for \(d' \leqslant d\).

Demonstration. Assume 3.4 (d') for \(d' < d\). By induction, we can assume that 4.3 (d') is true for \(d' < d\). Let the data be \(X'\), \(f\), \(U\) as in statement 4.3 (d). Then \(X'\) is strictly localized of an algebraic scheme on a field \(k\) at a closed geometric point \(x_o\). We can take \(X_o\) affine and of dimension \(\leqslant d\), even if it means replacing it with a neighborhood of \(x_o\). Then \(X'\) is the projective limit of affine and slack schemes \(X_i\) above \(X_o\) (VIII 4.5).

Let \(F\) be a torsion beam on \(U\). It is necessary to prove that \(H^q(U,F) = 0\) if \(q > d\), and it is still sufficient to treat the case where \(F\) is constructible. Then the data \(f\), \(F\), \(U\) come from analogous data on one of \(X_i\) (IX 2.7). We will have a beam \(F_i\) on \(U_i = X_i - V(f_i)\) which "induces" \(F\) on \(U\). Since \(U = \varprojlim U_i\), we have
\[H^q(U,F) = \varinjlim H^q(U_i,F_i) \;.\]
It is therefore sufficient to demonstrate the following fact: Whatever \(i\), there exists a commutative diagram
\[\xymatrix{ U \ar[rr] \ar[dr] & & U'' \ar[dl] \\ & U_i & }\]

where \(U \to U_i\) is the given arrow, such that \(\operatorname{cd}(U'') \leqslant d\). Indeed, this will imply that each element of \(H^q(U_i,F_i)\), for \(q > d\), has a null image in \(H^q(U,F)\).

Suppose \(i = 0\), and consider the morphism \(g : X_o \to \mathbb{E}^1_k = Y_o\) given by \(f_o \in \Gamma(X_o, \mathcal{O}_{X_o})\). Let \(Y'\) be the strict localization of \(\mathbb{E}^1_k\) at a geometric point above the origin, therefore \(Y'\) is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, and let \(X'' = X_o \times_{Y_o} Y'\).

The morphism \(X' \to X_o\) is factorized by \(X''\) since \(X'\) is strictly local. Furthermore, the open \(U'' = X'' \times_{X_o} U_o\) is none other than the generic fiber of \(X''/Y'\). It is therefore an affine algebraic scheme above the generic point of \(Y'\), and we obviously have \(\dim U'' \leqslant d-1\).

Let's check that \(\operatorname{cd} U'' \leqslant d\) (which will complete the demonstration). Let \(K\) be the residual body of \(Y'\) at the generic point. It is a body of cohomological dimension \(1\), that is to say, we have \(\operatorname{cd}(G) = 1\), \(G\) the Galois group of the separable closure \(\bar{K}\) of \(K\) (X 2.2). Or \(\bar{U}'' = U'' \times_{\operatorname{Spec} K} (\operatorname{Spec} \bar{K})\). We have \(\operatorname{cd} \bar{U}'' \leqslant d-1\) by hypothesis of recurrence on \(d\) (this is 2.2 for a diagram of dimension \(\leqslant d\)). The fact that \(\operatorname{cd} U'' \leqslant d\) then follows from the Hochschild-Serre spectral sequence (VIII 8.4)
\[H^p(G, H^q(\bar{U}'',\bar{F})) \Longrightarrow H^n(U'',F) \;,\]
hence Lemma 4.5.
